what_it_does: Presents a pre-rerun gap analysis of the deterministic extraction pass, separating verified-real gaps from dragnet noise and artifacts and proposing a fix sequence.
observations: Labels every major claim as VERIFIED-REAL, dragnet-noise, or own-artifact, and includes a retracted claim (G-ARTIFACT-1).
States that dragnet reliability is language-dependent: trustworthy for JS/JSX, noisy for Python, mixed for TS/TSX.
Reports that .jsx files have zero stored symbols and .js capture is largely empty, while .ts/.tsx extraction is actually good.
Finds that Python function/class extraction is sound and the headline '1,032 missing py functions' is dragnet noise.
Identifies runtime/suite.py as a 945 KB void because it exceeds the size guard, despite being the most-imported file.
Diagnoses a half-edge/resolution gap: most calls, imports, references, and all endpoint/event edges fail to resolve their far ends.
Treats staleness as an architectural problem requiring an incremental, source-hash-driven ledger rather than a one-time rerun.
Grounds the adjudication in a durable verifier (ops/verify_extraction/) using Python ast and TypeScript compiler API.
Ends with a five-step rerun sequence ordered by cost and leverage.
standouts: The dragnet must be read inversely to extractor quality: believe JS/JSX flags, verify Python flags, and treat TS/TSX as unknown.
The most-imported kernel file is entirely absent from the graph due to a size guard.
The real frontend extraction hole is JS/JSX, not TS/TSX — a mid-document self-correction.
The deepest issue is not a bug but architectural: a static ledger cannot coordinate live building.
conventions: Uses structured issue IDs (R1-R5, G-NOISE-1, G-ARTIFACT-1) with explicit validity labels.
Mixes prose, data tables, and a numbered action sequence.
Cites exact counts, commit dates, and verification tools throughout.
concerns: The durable verifier cannot automatically separate staleness from extractor bugs without a --at-commit mode.
TS/TSX flags remain uncategorized because the needed AST parser is missing.
The analysis admits several under-covered areas (non-code files, possible double-indexing).
The rerun sequence places the architectural staleness fix last even though it is called the deepest issue.
notes: A self-critical, evidence-calibrated audit that tells the team which dragnet alarms are real, which are noise, and in what order to fix them.
questions: What exact commit or hash corresponds to the June-27 extraction snapshot?
Which 63 new files were never indexed?
What is the current _MAX_TEXT_DEEP value and the chunking implementation status?
What in the dragnet prompt or schema causes Python over-flagging?
What is the intended source-hash-driven incremental refresh architecture?
purpose_vs_actual: Matches — it delivers a calibrated gap analysis, distinguishes verified-real gaps from noise, and proposes a fix-and-rerun sequence.
apparent_themes: Static self-model versus live system tension.
intention_signals: To produce a trustworthy, self-checked roadmap for the deterministic rerun by filtering dragnet noise, surfacing real extraction gaps, and pushing toward a live, resolved code graph.
novelty: Its explicit validity map inverts trust in the dragnet based on per-language extractor quality, and it publicly retracts/corrects its own over-broad claims.
contribution: A calibrated, evidence-annotated classification of extraction gaps and a language-specific reliability model that a fused system can use to prioritize fixes and avoid false-positive dragnet flags.
summary_for_embedding: A July 2026 pre-rerun gap analysis of the deterministic extraction pass that separates verified-real gaps (JS/JSX blindness, narrow symbol vocabulary, oversized-file void, unresolved half-edges, staleness) from dragnet noise, validates claims with AST ground truth, and proposes a fix-and-rerun sequence.
intention_for_embedding: To guide the deterministic extraction rerun by identifying which coverage findings are real, which are artifacts, and what tooling and architectural changes are needed.
